title: Getting Started
description: Scaffold a Howl project in 30 seconds and run it locally

blocks.text: Howl runs on Deno 2.x. There is no Node.js, no npm, and no global CLI to install — Deno fetches everything on demand.
The fastest way to start a Howl project is the official scaffolder. It is published to JSR and runs straight from the registry — no install, no clone.
deno run -Ar jsr:@hushkey/howl-init

You will be asked for a project name and shown a list of templates to pick from. Each template ships with a working dev/build/start task and an import map already wired to the latest @hushkey/howl version.

rows: Minimal API-only project — no client, no styling. Ships ping (GET) + pong (POST with requestBody) as starter routes
Minimal fullstack starter — 1 API, 1 page, 1 island, 1 signal-based component, Tailwind v4 + daisyUI
Tailwind v4 + daisyUI docs site driven by JSON manifests (this site is built from this template)
Tailwind v4 + daisyUI developer CV / portfolio with project cards and per-project detail pages

blocks.text: Skip the picker by passing the template id and project name as flags:
deno run -Ar jsr:@hushkey/howl-init my-app --template docs
The scaffolder keeps its templates in lockstep with the published @hushkey/howl version, so a fresh project always lines up with the docs you are reading.
Once the scaffolder finishes, jump in and start the dev server.

cd my-app
deno task dev
The dev server boots HowlBuilder, runs esbuild for islands and CSS, watches the file system, and serves on http://localhost:8000 by default. Edit any file under pages/ or apis/ and the page reloads.
Howl is convention over configuration — the file system itself is the router. Both shipped templates use the split-folder layout, with server-side code under server/ and pages/islands under client/:

my-app/
  server/
    apis/
      public/
        ping.api.ts      ← GET /api/public/ping
    main.ts              ← Howl app entry — middleware + routes
  client/
    pages/
      _app.tsx           ← HTML shell (head, scripts)
      _layout.tsx        ← shared layout for nested routes
      index.tsx          ← /
  static/                  ← served at /static/* (and root)
  howl.config.ts           ← shared State, roles, defineApi
  dev.ts                   ← dev server entry (HowlBuilder)
  deno.json                ← imports, tasks, compilerOptions
Single-folder apps work too — drop the server/ and client/ wrappers and put apis/, pages/, and main.ts at the project root. The framework infers the layout from how you wire HowlBuilder.

sections.heading: Adding Howl to an existing Deno project

blocks.text: If you already have a Deno project, add Howl from JSR. The scaffolder is the recommended path for greenfield apps; this is for grafting onto something existing.
deno add jsr:@hushkey/howl
Then wire up your entry. A minimal main.ts looks like this:
When grafting Howl into an existing app, make sure compilerOptions.jsx is "react-jsx" and jsxImportSource is "preact" in your deno.json. The scaffolder sets this for you automatically.
The shared config defines your State type, roles, and exports a typed defineApi helper. It is the single place to wire auth, caching, and rate limiting.
All scaffolded templates include a build task that produces a single bundled entry under dist/.

deno task build
deno task start
The docs template adds a compile task that produces a self-contained binary via deno compile, useful for shipping to bare metal or Docker:
deno task compile

items: Routing — file-system pages, layouts, and partial navigation
API Routes — typed endpoints with Zod validation and OpenAPI generation
Islands — selective hydration for interactive components
